abstract: The California E-Bike Incentive Project provides up to $2,000 in voucher incentives to low-income California residents for the purchase of electric bikes, including cargo and adaptive e-bikes. It also commits a portion of the funding to e-bike safety and education training, which is part of the application process before purchasing an e-bike.

For more details about the California program, see our FAQs below or visit the program website: ebikeincentives.org.

abstract: The California E-Bike Incentive Project is a statewide consumer-facing incentive program that aims to provide point-of-sale voucher incentives for the purchase of electric bicycles (e-bikes) to residents in disadvantaged communities, low-income communities, and low-income households with an annual household income of less than 300% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). All California-based e-bike retailers/brands are welcome to apply to take part in the program.

abstract: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is offering $2 million or approximately 1,000 vouchers to eligible applicants who will be selected at random to help ensure a fair distribution of vouchers. The project will continue to collaborate and partner with more than 20 community-based organizations statewide. These organizations will provide outreach and application assistance to low-income residents across California to empower and inform hard-to-reach applicants who encounter additional barriers to program participation such as lack of access to a computer or high-speed internet.

claim_text: There are equity gaps in California's current approach to incentivizing electric bike purchases.
title: California puts the brakes on E-Bike vouchers, transfers $18M
abstract: The California Air Resources Board said it has ended, for now, a popular program that provides vouchers for low-income Californians to purchase electric bicycles. Instead, about $18 million from the California E-Bike Incentive Project is being redirected to another incentive program called Clean Cars 4 All that helps people trade in their gas-guzzling cars for electric or hybrid vehicles, CARB confirmed. CARB said the e-bike incentive program, which launched in 2024, has helped more than 2,100 Californians with low incomes purchase an e-bike with up to $2,000 in vouchers. The vouchers were doled out to eligible recipients during two application periods.

abstract: California is making it easier for lower-income residents to purchase e-bikes by offering up to $2,000 in point-of-sale incentives. The program is focused on equity, aiming to make e-bikes accessible to those who need them most. Eligible applicants must be 18 or older, live in California, and meet certain income limits. Priority is given to people in low-income and disadvantaged communities. The next application window opens April 29, 2025.

title: Just Launched: California E-Bike Incentive Program
abstract: The California E-Bike Incentive Project has officially launched, providing up to $2,000 of point-of-sale incentives to support the purchase of a new electric bicycle. This initial phase will allow for the purchase of 1,500 e-bikes across California on a first-come, first-serve basis, and the program is set to expand later in 2025. Income-qualified approved applicants will receive a base voucher of $1,750 and an additional $250 for accessories, which can be applied when purchasing an e-bike, meaning no upfront costs for recipients.

claim_text: The funding will help transform the Port into a zero-emission hub.
title: Optimal Management of a Smart Port with Shore-Connection and Hydrogen Supplying by Stochastic Model Predictive Control
abstract: This paper proposes an optimal management strategy for a Smart Port equipped with renewable generation, including an electrified quay operating Cold-Ironing and a Hydrogen-based quay supplying Zero-Emission Ships. The strategy utilizes Battery Energy Storage and Hydrogen Energy Storage Systems to manage renewable energy sources and supply electric and hydrogen-fueled ships. A model predictive control algorithm is designed to define the best economic strategy during operations, considering uncertainties in renewable energy generation through stochastic optimization. The approach is tested on a potential future Smart Port equipped with wind and photovoltaic generation.
authors: Francesco Conte; Fabio D'Agostino; Daniele Kaza; Stefano Massucco; Gianluca Natrella; Federico Silvestro

claim_text: The funding will help transform the Port into a zero-emission hub.
title: Artificial Intelligence in Port Logistics: A Bibliometric Analysis of Technological Integration and Research Dynamics
abstract: This paper explores the transformation of port logistics operations with artificial intelligence during the port transformation into a smart port. The research integrates capabilities-based resource analysis and dynamic capabilities with sociotechnical implementations of technologies and resilience approaches of complex systems under disruptions. The system applies robust data infrastructures to propel analytical and AI modules that become effective once integrated with sufficient governance systems and trained personnel and operational processes to transform planning and safety and sustainability operations. It applies Scopus bibliometric research to analyze 123 articles using a systematic approach with both a search protocol and a document screening and duplication verification. It incorporates annual behavior and distribution of author and country performance analysis with science mapping techniques that explore keyword relation and co-citation and bibliographic coupling and conceptual structuring tools that construct thematic maps and multiple correspondence analysis with community detection while applying explicit thresholding and robust tests. The research connects AI applications to smart port domains through specific data-to-impact pathways while providing a method for bibliometric analysis that enables future updates. The research presents a step-by-step approach for data readiness followed by predictive and optimization implementation and organizational integration. The paper supports public policy through recommendations for data sharing standards and complete environmental benefit assessments. The research proposes a future study plan which combines field-based testing with multiple port assessments to enhance both cause-effect understanding and research applicability.
authors: Abdelhafid Khazzar; Yassine Sekaki; Yasser Lachhab; Said El-marzouki

title: Reducing carbon emissions in the dry port-seaport system: a shared transport approach
abstract: This research analyses the carbon emissions associated with a shared transport method within the dry port-seaport network, focusing on the realm of green transportation. Utilizing theoretical analysis and numerical examples, the study contrasts carbon emission levels across three distinct scenarios: shared transport, non-shared transport, and direct road transport. Numerical investigations are conducted to assess the environmental value sensitivity. Results indicate that when the total distance traveled by shippers exceeds that of shared transport, the milk-run transport approach can markedly decrease carbon emissions. Furthermore, transitioning from unimodal road transport to intermodal transport has the potential to yield significant carbon emission savings. This study offers a validated policy objective for fostering a more sustainable transportation system by promoting a modal shift from direct to shared transport.

claim_text: The funding will help transform the Port into a zero-emission hub.
title: Green innovation in ports: drivers, domains, and challenges
abstract: The transition toward sustainable maritime development has placed ports at the forefront of global green transformation. As critical nodes in maritime supply chains, ports are under growing pressure to reduce emissions, optimize resource use, and adopt digital technologies. While numerous green practices have emerged in recent years, the literature on port green innovation remains fragmented, lacking a comprehensive synthesis of its drivers, application domains, and systemic challenges. To address this gap, this study conducts a systematic review guided by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) framework, drawing on peer-reviewed publications from the Scopus and Web of Science databases. The analysis identifies three principal drivers of green innovation in ports: internal dynamics stemming from economic and technological capabilities, external pressures related to environmental regulation and policy, and societal expectations that steer transformation. Green innovation practices are classified into four functional domains: foundational measures, operational optimization, energy system transformation, and intelligent upgrading. In parallel, four major challenges are revealed: persistent ecological pressures, energy transition bottlenecks, fragmented regulatory frameworks, and operational trade-offs. This review contributes an integrated analytical framework that clarifies the conceptual landscape of port green innovation and offers actionable insights for research, policy, and strategic planning.
authors: Ying Liu; Yiying Chao; Shangwei Xie; Guofeng Wang; Lu Wang; Cheng Xue

claim_text: The funding will help transform the Port into a zero-emission hub.
title: Roadmap to a zero-emission port: A case study in Port of Yangpu
abstract: This briefing addresses the urgent need to decarbonize ports, focusing on the Port of Yangpu in Hainan, China. Despite China being home to seven of the top 10 global container ports, the paper highlights a lack of climate ambitions among these ports. The Port of Yangpu, anticipating significant growth in ship traffic, is presented with an opportunity to develop a decarbonization strategy. The analysis outlines three scenarios—Announced Ambitions, Partial Decarbonization, and Full Decarbonization—to assess technological roadmaps for achieving zero well-to-wake (WTW) carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e) emissions by 2050. While the Announced Ambitions scenario falls short, the more aggressive Partial and Full Decarbonization scenarios, relying on electrification and zero-emission fuels, offer potential solutions, with the Full Decarbonization scenario achieving zero emissions by 2050. The case study acknowledges varying costs associated with these technological pathways and suggests that economic benefits and environmental advantages could outweigh these costs. The briefing emphasizes integrating decarbonization plans into development strategies, refining roadmaps based on cost-effectiveness, and early planning for achieving decarbonization goals. The analysis stresses the necessity for the Port of Yangpu to adopt electrification and zero-emission fuels to meet ambitious decarbonization goals by 2050 and cautions against relying on fossil liquified natural gas as a bridging fuel solution given the issue of methane slip with marine engines.

claim_text: Crisis Pregnancy Centers that offer or refer for abortion pill reversal are part of the new frontier of the anti-abortion movement.
title: Crisis Pregnancy Centers in the United States: Lack of Adherence to Medical and Ethical Practice Standards; A Joint Position Statement of the Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine and the North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology
abstract: Crisis pregnancy centers (CPCs) attempt to dissuade pregnant people from considering abortion, often using misinformation and unethical practices. While mimicking health care clinics, CPCs provide biased, limited, and inaccurate health information, including incomplete pregnancy options counseling and unscientific sexual and reproductive health information. The centers do not provide or refer for abortion or contraception but often advertise in ways that give the appearance that they do provide these services without disclosing the biased nature and marked limitations of their services. Although individuals working in CPCs in the United States have First Amendment rights to free speech, their provision of misinformation might be harmful to young people and adults. The Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine and North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology support the following positions: (1) CPCs pose risk by failing to adhere to medical and ethical practice standards; (2) governments should only support health programs that provide accurate, comprehensive information; (3) CPCs and individuals who provide CPC services should be held to established standards of ethics and medical care; (4) schools should not outsource sexual education to CPCs or other entities that do not provide accurate and complete health information; (5) search engines and digital platforms should enforce policies against misleading advertising by CPCs; and (6) health professionals should educate themselves, and young people about CPCs and help young people identify safe, quality sources of sexual and reproductive health information and care.
authors: Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine; North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology

claim_text: Crisis Pregnancy Centers that offer or refer for abortion pill reversal are part of the new frontier of the anti-abortion movement.
title: Crisis Pregnancy Centers in the United States: Post-Dobbs Characteristics and Changes
abstract: This study enumerates crisis pregnancy centers (CPCs) in the United States in 2024 and examines changes before and after the Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization decision. The study found that in 2024, 2,633 CPCs operated in the United States, a 3.3% increase from 2021. Nationally, 71.7% offered nondiagnostic ultrasounds, 28.0% advertised sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing, 17.2% STI treatment, 6.8% HIV testing, and 3.8% unproven 'abortion reversal' on-site. The total count of CPCs in 2024 was 230% greater in states with a grant program, and the rate of newly identified and re-opened CPCs was 1.81 times greater, adjusting for CPCs in 2021.

claim_text: SB 887 accelerates the construction of transmission needed to deliver new clean energy resources in California.
title: SB 887
abstract: California Senate Bill 887 (SB 887) aims to accelerate the planning and approval of new transmission facilities to support the state's clean energy goals, including achieving 100% renewable or zero-carbon electricity by 2045. The bill requires the Public Utilities Commission (PUC) and the California Energy Commission (CEC) to provide long-term forecasts to the California Independent System Operator (CAISO) that extend at least 15 years into the future, focusing on reducing reliance on gas plants due to transmission bottlenecks and aligning with the state's offshore wind strategy. Additionally, SB 887 directs the PUC to ask CAISO to consider approving high-priority, long-lead-time projects during its 2022-23 transmission planning process based on existing information.

claim_text: SB 887 accelerates the construction of transmission needed to deliver new clean energy resources in California.
title: SENATE COMMITTEE ON ENERGY, UTILITIES AND
abstract: The Senate Committee on Energy, Utilities and Communications' analysis of SB 887 highlights the urgency of building transmission capacity to meet California's clean energy goals. The analysis emphasizes that major new transmission projects often take 10 years or more to build, and without comparable efforts to expand transmission capacity, the state's clean energy transition could fail to meet its climate objectives. SB 887 aims to ensure that transmission development is tackled in a timely and cost-effective manner, preventing it from becoming a barrier to the state's clean energy transition.

claim_text: SB 887 accelerates the construction of transmission needed to deliver new clean energy resources in California.
title: Understanding California’s Transmission Development Delays and Paths to Reform
abstract: This report by the Union of Concerned Scientists examines the delays in California's transmission development and proposes reforms to accelerate the integration of renewable energy resources. It underscores the necessity of expanding transmission capacity to connect renewable energy sources to urban centers and meet the state's clean energy goals. The report also discusses the challenges posed by insufficient transmission capacity, which has led to grid stress and delays in retiring fossil fuel power plants, and recommends strategies to streamline transmission development processes.

claim_text: SB 887 accelerates the construction of transmission needed to deliver new clean energy resources in California.
title: Carbon Emission Flow Tracing: Fast Algorithm and California Grid Study
abstract: This study presents a novel and computationally efficient approach for quantifying nodal average and marginal carbon emission rates in power systems, applicable to both AC and DC optimal power flow problems. The approach is validated through simulations of the 8,870-bus realistic California grid using actual CAISO data, providing fine-grained spatiotemporal emission analysis for every California county. The study offers insights into how emissions are spatially distributed under varying system conditions, which is crucial for understanding the impact of transmission infrastructure on carbon emissions.
authors: Yuqing Shen; Yuanyuan Shi; Daniel Kirschen; Yize Chen

claim_text: SB 887 accelerates the construction of transmission needed to deliver new clean energy resources in California.
title: California Test System (CATS): A Geographically Accurate Test System based on the California Grid
abstract: This paper introduces the California Test System (CATS), a synthetic transmission grid in California designed for power systems policy research without revealing critical energy information. The system combines publicly available geographic data of California's electric infrastructure with invented topology and transmission line parameters that are realistic but not real. CATS is suitable for power flow and policy analyses with geo-referenced applications, including studies related to weather, topography, and socio-economic considerations.
authors: Sofia Taylor; Aditya Rangarajan; Noah Rhodes; Jonathan Snodgrass; Bernie Lesieutre; Line A. Roald

claim_text: SB 887 accelerates the construction of transmission needed to deliver new clean energy resources in California.
title: Optimal transmission expansion minimally reduces decarbonization costs of U.S. electricity
abstract: This study analyzes optimal transmission expansion jointly with storage, generation, and hourly operations across the three primary interconnects in the United States. The findings indicate that while transmission expansion offers more benefits in a high-renewable system, it decreases the cost of a 100% clean system by only 4% compared to a plan that relies solely on current transmission. Expanding capacity within existing interconnects can achieve most of these savings, suggesting that transmission expansion alone may not be sufficient to minimize decarbonization costs.
authors: Rangrang Zheng; Greg Schivley; Patricia Hidalgo-Gonzalez; Matthias Fripp; Michael J. Roberts

claim_text: SB 887 accelerates the construction of transmission needed to deliver new clean energy resources in California.
title: Reducing transmission expansion by co-optimizing sizing of wind, solar, storage and grid connection capacity
abstract: This paper develops functionalities to explore the substitutability of storage for transmission and the optimal capacity and siting decisions of renewable energy and battery resources through 2030 in the Western Interconnection of the United States. The findings indicate that optimizing interconnection capacity and co-locating storage with renewable energy resources can reduce total grid connection and shorter-distance transmission capacity expansion by approximately 10%, highlighting the potential of storage to alleviate transmission expansion needs.
authors: Aneesha Manocha; Gabriel Mantegna; Neha Patankar; Jesse D. Jenkins

claim_text: Nearly 65% of transition-age youth in foster care reported having lost their jobs, with almost a quarter (23%) at risk of losing their housing.
title: Transition from care: status and outcomes of youth who age out of foster care
abstract: This study shows that a significant portion of youth exiting the foster care system face serious difficulty transitioning to life on their own. Many live on the streets, lack the money to meet basic living expenses, fail to maintain regular employment, are involved with the criminal justice system, are unable to obtain health care, and experience early pregnancies. Although youth reported exposure to independent living training while in care, few reported concrete assistance. Multiple placements while in care and less education correlated with more difficult postdischarge functioning. Training, services, positive supportive networks, and job experience in care are associated with more positive adjustments.
venue: Child and Adolescent Social Work Journal
authors: Courtney, M. E.; Dworsky, A.; Lee, J. S.; Raap, M.; Bost, N.

claim_text: Nearly 65% of transition-age youth in foster care reported having lost their jobs, with almost a quarter (23%) at risk of losing their housing.
title: Homelessness during the transition from foster care to adulthood
abstract: Youths aging out of foster care are at high risk for becoming homeless during the transition to adulthood. Between 31% and 46% of our study participants had been homeless at least once by age 26 years. Running away while in foster care, greater placement instability, being male, having a history of physical abuse, engaging in more delinquent behaviors, and having symptoms of a mental health disorder were associated with an increase in the relative risk of becoming homeless.
authors: Dworsky, A.; Fusaro, V. A.; Fisher, P. A.; Pecora, P. J.

claim_text: Nearly 65% of transition-age youth in foster care reported having lost their jobs, with almost a quarter (23%) at risk of losing their housing.
title: Effects of individual risk and state housing factors on adverse outcomes in a national sample of youth transitioning out of foster care
abstract: Approximately 30% of the variation in each of the 19-year-old outcomes could be attributed to state-level effects. Residence in a state that spent above average of CFCIP budget on housing supports reduced the risk of homelessness and incarceration. Living in a state with a higher proportion of housing-burdened low-income renters significantly increased the risk of substance abuse and child birth. Individual-level risks were significant: racial/ethnic minority, male gender, past risk history, placement instability, child behavioral problems, residence in group home or runaway. Remaining in foster care at age 19 reduced the odds of homelessness, incarceration, and substance abuse.
authors: Courtney, M. E.; Dworsky, A.; Lee, J. S.; Terao, S.; Bost, N.

title: A Wave of Resignations in the Aftermath of Remote Onboarding
abstract: This study investigates employee resignation patterns at Ericsson, analyzing how different work modalities influence employee retention. Findings show a marked increase in resignations among employees with less than five years of tenure, particularly those onboarded remotely during the pandemic, even after returning to the office. The study emphasizes the importance of employee integration practices in hybrid environments to sustain retention in knowledge-intensive companies.
authors: Darja Smite; Franz Zieris; Lars-Ola Damm

title: The role of work-life balance in effective business management
abstract: This study examines the role of work-life balance (WLB) as a strategic component of effective business management and its influence on employee motivation, job satisfaction, and organizational performance. Findings reveal that flexible working arrangements are the most impactful tool for enhancing work-life balance and significantly contribute to higher levels of employee motivation. The study emphasizes that organizations actively supporting WLB achieve greater employee loyalty, improved productivity, and enhanced employer branding.

title: The Daily Life of Software Engineers during the COVID-19 Pandemic
abstract: This study investigates how the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent lockdowns have impacted software engineers' daily routines, well-being, and productivity. Findings suggest that working remotely is not inherently a challenge for organizations or developers, as the time spent on specific activities from home was similar to that in the office. The study concludes that remote work does not pose significant challenges for organizations or developers.
authors: Daniel Russo; Paul P. H. Hanel; Seraphina Altnickel; Niels van Berkel

title: Pandemic Programming: How COVID-19 affects software developers and how their organizations can help
abstract: This study investigates the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic on software developers' well-being and productivity. Findings indicate that the pandemic has had a negative effect on developers' well-being and productivity, with factors such as disaster preparedness, fear related to the pandemic, and home office ergonomics affecting well-being and productivity. The study suggests that to improve employee productivity, software companies should focus on maximizing employee well-being and improving the ergonomics of employees' home offices.

The top marginal rate on wage income would soar to 18.05 percent—nationally, the median top marginal rate is 5.3 percent—and the state would adopt a new 2.3 percent gross receipts tax (GRT), at a rate more than three times that of the country’s highest current pure GRT.

abstract: Taxes on California businesses and consumers would be increased a stunning $163 billion annually under disastrous legislation introduced this week by Assemblymember Ash Kalra (San Jose) to fund a single-payer healthcare system that has been rejected multiple times as unworkable, impractical and too costly. The Bay Area Council has long been a strong advocate for reforming our healthcare system to improve quality, access and outcomes while also reducing costs for business and consumers, and we were an outlier among business groups in supporting Affordable Care Act reforms. But replacing a free-market system with a government-run program isn’t the answer, even if you can somehow forget the state’s middling track record in effectively and efficiently managing and operating large, highly complex projects.

title: Impacts on U.S. Energy Markets and the Economy of Reducing Oil Imports
abstract: This report analyzes the economic consequences of reducing oil imports in the United States, focusing on alternative world crude oil price trajectories and the use of an oil import fee. It finds that higher world oil prices lead to increased domestic production and constrained demand, with each successive reduction of one million barrels per day of oil imports achieved at progressively higher costs. The report also examines the macroeconomic impacts of implementing an oil import fee, noting that while it generates substantial revenues, the overall economic effects are complex and depend on various factors.

title: Drug & Alcohol Overdose Hospitalization & Death in Orange County
abstract: This report provides a summary of the leading causes of substance-related overdoses in Orange County, highlighting a dramatic rise from 2019 to 2020, with a 45% increase in deaths involving opioids, attributed to the increased presence of fentanyl in the county, state, and nation.

title: Opioid Overdose & Death in Orange County
abstract: This report summarizes opioid-related morbidity and mortality in Orange County, noting that from 2017 to 2021, the rate of death due to opioid overdose nearly tripled, from 7.9 deaths per 100,000 to 23.2, with the largest increase occurring in 2020, coinciding with the COVID-19 pandemic.

claim_text: In Orange County, there was a 162% increase in fentanyl related deaths from 2019 to 2020.

abstract: This study presents a two-year review of fentanyl concentrations in driving under the influence and postmortem cases in Orange County, CA, aiming to support the interpretation of fentanyl concentrations in postmortem cases and aid in the determination of the cause of death.
authors: D. M. Parker; J. M. Buchanich; M. S. Roberts; P. Kumar; J. M. Buchanich

title: Sociodemographic and geographic disparities in excess fatal drug overdoses during the COVID-19 pandemic in California: A population-based study
abstract: This study estimates excess fatal drug overdoses in California during the COVID-19 pandemic, finding a 44% increase over the same period one year prior, with excess deaths driven by opioids, especially synthetic opioids, and disproportionately affecting non-Hispanic Black and Other non-Hispanic populations.
authors: A. M. Buchanich; M. S. Roberts; P. Kumar; J. M. Buchanich

title: Opioid crisis: Big increase in CA fentanyl overdoses
abstract: This article discusses the exponential rise in fentanyl-related deaths in California since 2019, noting that between September 2021 and September 2022, 5,942 people died after ingesting the synthetic opioid, accounting for about 86% of all opioid-related deaths in the state.

claim_text: An investigation found that Governor Newsom overstated the number of acres treated with fuel breaks and prescribed burns by 690%.
title: Newsom credibility hurt by wildfire prevention claims
abstract: An investigation found that Governor Newsom overstated the number of acres treated with fuel breaks and prescribed burns by 690%, claiming 90,000 acres when only 11,399 acres were treated.

claim_text: An investigation found that Governor Newsom overstated the number of acres treated with fuel breaks and prescribed burns by 690%.
title: Newsom Misled the Public About Wildfire Prevention Efforts Ahead Of Worst Fire Season On Record
abstract: An investigation found that Governor Newsom overstated, by an astounding 690%, the number of acres treated with fuel breaks and prescribed burns in the very forestry projects he said needed to be prioritized to protect the state’s most vulnerable communities.

claim_text: The actual number of acres treated as a result of Governor Newsom's executive order was only 11,399 acres, contrary to his claim of 90,000 acres.
title: Newsom Misled the Public About Wildfire Prevention Efforts Ahead of Worst Fire Season on Record
abstract: An analysis reveals that Governor Newsom's administration overstated the acreage treated under priority wildfire prevention projects, claiming 90,000 acres when only 11,399 acres were actually treated, raising concerns about transparency and effectiveness in wildfire mitigation efforts.

claim_text: The actual number of acres treated as a result of Governor Newsom's executive order was only 11,399 acres, contrary to his claim of 90,000 acres.
title: Report: Newsom's claimed success on wildfire prevention missed the mark – by 690%
abstract: A report indicates that Governor Newsom's administration exaggerated the success of wildfire prevention efforts, claiming treatment of 90,000 acres when only 11,399 acres were addressed, highlighting significant discrepancies in reported outcomes.
